summary: Mitchell Hashimoto shares his methodology for completing large technical projects, centered on maintaining motivation through continuous visible progress. Rather than planning everything upfront or building to perfection, he advocates decomposing projects into small pieces that yield tangible results quickly. Using his terminal emulator as an example, he demonstrates how prioritizing demos over finished components, embracing 'good enough' implementations, and becoming your own first user creates a sustainable feedback loop. The approach acknowledges that perfectionism often derails projects, and that seeing working code—even imperfect code—is what keeps engineers engaged over months of development.
key_points: Break large projects into sub-components where you can see results as soon as possible, not into comprehensive task lists
Automated tests provide early visible progress when building non-graphical components—watching test counts grow creates excitement
Sprint to demos rather than finished components; do not let perfection or known future improvements stop forward progress
Senior engineers often get bogged down building the perfect implementation only to discover the product itself sucks
Adopt your own software as quickly as possible and build only what you need—personal investment in the problem drives motivation
money_quotes: We've all experienced that feeling of excitement starting a new project. The first few weeks you can't wait to get on the computer to work. Then slowly over time you get distracted or make up excuses and work on it less.
As a broad generalization I've not found an engineer who doesn't get excited by a good demo. And the goal is to always give yourself a good demo.
Do not let perfection be an enemy of progress. Going further, do not let future improvements you know you'll have to make stop you from moving on to the next thing.

I've seen senior engineers get bogged down building the perfect thing and by the time they get a demo, they realize it sucks. The implementation doesn't suck, but the product or feature itself actually sucks.
Seeing the progression of '1 test passed', '4 tests passed,' '13 tests passed' and so on is super exciting to me. I'm running some code I wrote and it's working.
I always feel a lot of pride using software with code that I wrote and that usually helps keep me motivated to continue working on it.
I don't think you need to ship a project for it to be successful.
key_insight: Sustained motivation on large projects comes from optimizing for continuous visible progress through demos and working tests, not from comprehensive planning or building perfect components.
